data_flow_trace.original: GOVERNANCE calls setForNextEpoch(...) with autoEvictEnabled=true and autoEvictThreshold=type(uint256).max; _validateConfig does not bound autoEvictThreshold.
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: The 8th parameter is now `uint64 _autoEvictThresholdPct` (a percentage) instead of `uint256 _autoEvictThreshold` (a raw proposal count). _validateConfig is called at :200-207 and explicitly bounds the threshold at :301-303 — `if (_autoEvictThresholdPct > 100) revert Errors.InvalidAutoEvictThresholdPct(...)`.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Any value > 100 reverts. Even uint64.max input cannot bypass (compiler rejects uint256.max at ABI level; any value in (100, uint64.max] reverts in _validateConfig).
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Field type changed from `uint256 autoEvictThreshold` to `uint64 autoEvictThresholdPct`; new validator enforces [0,100]; the legacy uint256 field is retained only as `__deprecated_autoEvictThreshold` for storage-layout compatibility (always written as 0 — see :217).

data_flow_trace.original: Pending struct written with unbounded threshold; hasPendingConfig=true.
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: PendingConfig is still written and hasPendingConfig set to true, but the written autoEvictThresholdPct has already passed the 0..100 validation; __deprecated_autoEvictThreshold is hard-coded to 0.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Must pass _validateConfig first; unreachable with malicious threshold.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Struct now carries autoEvictThresholdPct (bounded) plus a dead __deprecated_autoEvictThreshold (always 0).

data_flow_trace.original: applyPendingConfig copies autoEvictThreshold to live storage.
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: applyPendingConfig now copies autoEvictThresholdPct (line 248). The deprecated raw threshold is never copied/read.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Only RECONFIGURATION may call; hasPendingConfig must be true.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Copies the bounded pct, not a uint256 raw count.

data_flow_trace.original: ValidatorManagement loop tests `perfs[i].successfulProposals <= threshold`; threshold=uint256.max makes the test true for all validators.
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: The loop now reads `autoEvictThresholdPct` (uint64, already bounded to [0,100] at write-time) at :670. The comparison at :705-706 is `successPct = (successfulProposals * 100) / total; if (successPct < thresholdPct) shouldEvict = true`. There is no `<= uint256.max` path — a threshold above 100 can never be stored.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Eviction only occurs for validators with strict success percentage below the configured (0..100) threshold; zero-proposal or zero-success validators are evicted regardless, but that is an orthogonal, intentional rule.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Absolute-count comparison replaced with percentage comparison; threshold domain shrunk from uint256 to [0,100]; semantics reversed (evict if BELOW threshold, not AT/BELOW).

data_flow_trace.original: Every ACTIVE except last is set PENDING_INACTIVE in one tx — C6 violated.
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: The liveness-preserve check (`if (remainingActive <= 1) continue`) still exists at :712-715. With a well-behaved, correctly-configured network (validators averaging >= thresholdPct success), no mass eviction occurs. The attack's root cause — unbounded threshold covering all validators — is gone.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Even at the max legal value thresholdPct=100, only validators strictly below 100% success would be evicted. This is a tight but intentional admin choice, not an unbounded weapon.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: No way to craft a threshold that deterministically evicts every validator; the attacker's cudgel (uint256.max) is rejected in _validateConfig.

reasoning_chain: The reported vulnerability — governance sets autoEvictThreshold=uint256.max to mass-evict all validators — is rooted in (a) the threshold being an unbounded uint256 raw proposal count, (b) _validateConfig not checking it, and (c) the loop using `successfulProposals <= threshold`. The current code completely refactors this: the field is renamed to `autoEvictThresholdPct`, typed `uint64`, and _validateConfig at src/runtime/ValidatorConfig.sol:301-303 reverts on any value > 100. ValidatorManagement.sol:670 reads this bounded pct and at :705-706 compares `successPct < thresholdPct`. A malicious governance call cannot set a threshold that evicts all honest validators; at worst thresholdPct=100 evicts only those with <100% success, which is an operator-risk decision rather than an unbounded admin-rug. The original vector is fully closed.

key_evidence.why_matters: Explicit upper bound on the threshold that previously was unbounded — blocks the uint256.max input described in the report.
The raw-count threshold is explicitly marked deprecated and replaced by a percentage; storage layout is preserved only for upgrade safety.

key_evidence.excerpt: uint256 total = successfulProposals + failedProposals; ... uint256 successPct = (successfulProposals * 100) / total; if (successPct < thresholdPct) shouldEvict = true;
key_evidence.why_matters: Eviction criterion is now a percentage comparison against a bounded value; uint256.max is unrepresentable as a percentage and was already rejected at config-write time.
The deprecated uint256 field is hard-coded to 0 on every write — no path keeps the old unbounded threshold alive.

counterargument: One might argue that governance setting thresholdPct=100 still lets a malicious proposal mass-evict any validator whose success rate is <100%, which in a realistic network is almost everyone — effectively preserving the original attack with a one-epoch delay. However, (i) the report explicitly frames the issue as an unbounded uint256 allowing `successfulProposals <= uint256.max` to always hold; the current code has no such path. (ii) thresholdPct=100 is a deliberate, on-chain-visible governance knob with a bounded domain, not an overflow/missing-check bug. (iii) The liveness preserve at ValidatorManagement.sol:712-715 still spares at least one validator, and unbonded/pending_inactive validators can rejoin next epoch. The specific high-severity vector described — unbounded threshold causing deterministic mass eviction of an arbitrary active set — no longer exists in the code.
